exemplar.title: To what extent does household income level influence differences in electricity consumption behaviors in Surabaya, Indonesia?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does household income level influence differences in electricity consumption behaviors in Surabaya, Indonesia?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Uses appropriate technical terminology (ANOVA, Tukey HSD) consistently
Overall argument is logical and progresses from methods to conclusions
weaknesses: Formatting glitches, misplaced tables and varying citation styles disrupt flow
Malformed tables interrupt coherence and reader understanding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and organization, use of terminology, and logic all sit in the 1â2 band due to sustained but imperfect presentation. The work is organized and terms are correct, yet concision and coherence issues prevent a higher mark, resulting in 2.

strengths: Clear, coherent and focused research question directly linked to variables
Sustained discussion of global and local context narrowing to Surabayaâs electricity demand
Well-explained connection between income disparities and conservation policy
weaknesses: Minor wording redundancy in research question reduces precision
Context section drifts into generic climate facts rather than Surabaya-specific data
Connections to economic, social, and technological factors are described superficially
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands are firmly in the 5â6 band and one in the 3â4 band. The clear, testable research question and sustained contextual treatment merit the upper band. Minor issues in focus and supporting local evidence prevent a full 6, placing this criterion at 5.

strengths: Method design is repeatable and appropriate (Google FormâââANOVA)
Ethical considerations of consent and anonymity are outlined
Hypotheses and variable classification are clearly stated
weaknesses: Sampling strategy is un-justified convenience sampling with potential bias
Procedure omits data-cleaning protocol and handling of missing responses
Risk assessment is brief, lacking data-security measures and IRB approval details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design sits in the 3â4 band but sampling is fundamentally flawed (1â2 band) and risk treatment is limited (3â4 band). Balancing these, the overall planning performance matches the mid-band (3).

strengths: Evaluation links statistical outcomes to mitigation and policy implications
Method strengths and weaknesses are identified (repeatability, bias, scale issues)
Improvements proposed are meaningful (meter data, equal sample sizes)
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks external literature or policy trade-off discussion
Limitations are listed rather than critically analysed (e.g., confounding variables)
Suggested improvements are relevant but not sufficient to address major weaknesses fully
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands are descriptive with moderate depth, matching the 3â4 band at the lower end. The evaluation, method critique, and suggestions are present but not comprehensive enough for a higher mark, yielding a 3.

strengths: Appropriate choice of bar charts and Tukey HSD to display group differences
Correct application of ANOVA and interpretation of p-values
Conclusion relates observed trends to behavioural explanations
weaknesses: Multiple tables are malformed or contain placeholder text
Not all survey questions are fully presented; summary statistics are missing
No effect-size reporting or assumption checks documented
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and analysis are generally correct but incomplete, with omissions in tables and missing values. The conclusion is valid but limited by partial data support. All strands fit the mid-band descriptor (3â4); hence a score of 3.

strengths: Application proposal is clearly justified using study findings
Consideration of emissions impact and subsidy rationale is explicit
weaknesses: Evaluation of solution omits key factors (rebound effects, monitoring strategies)
Discussion of weaknesses is partial and lacks broader socio-economic considerations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application proposal meets the 3-mark descriptor, but the solution evaluation is only at the 2-mark level. With one strand in band 3 and one in band 2, the overall performance aligns with 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Technical terms such as ANOVA, Tukey HSD, p-value, independent/dependent variables are used correctly; however, the report is wordy and occasionally repetitive, so concision is lacking.  Meeting one of the two requirements corresponds to band 2.

The student discusses what the statistical outcomes imply for income-related behaviours and climate mitigation, but evaluation depth is moderate; no reference is made to external literature or broader policy trade-offs.  This equates to a contextual evaluation with omissions (3-4 band, score 3).

The conclusion restates significant and non-significant findings and links them to behavioural explanations, but support is limited by the partial data presentation and lacks quantitative effect sizes.  Nonetheless, it draws on observed trends and remains largely valid, satisfying the 3-4 descriptor at the lower score.

The student applies one-way ANOVA and interprets p-values, occasionally supplementing with Tukey tests.  However, several ANOVA tables contain nonsensical strings, and explanations of assumptions or effect sizes are absent.  Analysis is generally correct but incomplete, fitting mid-band (3).

Suggested improvementsâequal sample sizes, inclusion of metered consumption data, and broader behavioural questionsâare meaningful and address important weaknesses, yet they stop short of fully tackling sampling bias or statistical assumption checks.  Recommendations for future research are present but not âsignificantâ enough for the top band.

The procedure for creating and distributing a Google Form, converting responses to numerical scores, and analysing them with one-way ANOVA is replicable and appropriate for detecting mean differences.  Nevertheless, the method lacks detail on sample size determination, response rate management and treatment of missing data, so it cannot be judged fully sufficient; a solidâbut not exemplaryâdesign (3-4 band).

Connections between income-related behaviour and the broader environmental problem are described (e.g., higher-income groups overlook conservation, leading to greater emissions) and linked to the need for targeted policy.  However, the explanation is uneven; economic, social and technological factors are only superficially mentioned and supporting data are absent.  This equates to a competent description with some omissions, fitting the 3-4 band.

The research question is clearly stated, geographically and conceptually focused (income-related electricity behaviours in Surabaya) and easily testable through the chosen method.  It is expressed in a single, coherent sentence and aligns directly with the variables later defined.  Minor wording redundancy ("influence differences") slightly affects precision, but not coherence; therefore the work best fits the 5-6 band.

Strengths (universal applicability) and weaknesses (budget strain, limited effect on wealthy users) are discussed, but other factorsâmarket rebound effects, monitoring, unintended social impactsâare absent.  The evaluation is partial, aligning with the 2-mark level.

The IA follows the required ESS structure (introduction, methodology, data, eval, applications) but formatting glitches and misplaced tables interrupt the flow.  Organisation is present but not sustained flawlessly, meriting 2 marks.

A strengths/limitations table identifies repeatability, self-report bias, Likert scale issues and unequal group sizes.  Discussion is descriptive rather than critical, and some limitations (e.g., uncontrolled confounding variables) are merely listed.  This demonstrates description rather than exhaustive discussion; thus a 3.

The student explains global climate change, links it to fossil-fuel electricity generation, and narrows the discussion to Indonesia and Surabayaâs rising demand.  This provides clear contextual relevance for investigating household electricity use.  Although the discussion occasionally drifts into generic climate facts, it still constitutes a sustained, relevant treatment of the issue, meriting the upper band.

Sampling is essentially convenience based (teachers and friends sharing a link).  No justification of numbers, socio-demographic balance or stratification is attempted.  The strand requirement of âjustifying the choiceâ is therefore not met; errors and omissions predominate, placing it at the bottom of the 1-2 band.

A table identifies key ethical points (informed consent, anonymity) and minor environmental impact (online survey).  While these are relevant, the risk assessment is brief and excludes potential psychological discomfort or data-security procedures.  Thus risks are outlined rather than comprehensively described, matching the 3-4 band at the lower end.

Bar charts of Tukey HSD results and several tables are provided, but many tables are malformed or missing values.  Not all seven survey questions have clearly presented summary statistics.  Therefore diagrams are appropriate yet incomplete, aligning with âappropriate â¦ with some omissionsâ (3-4 band, lower end).

The proposal to subsidise energy-efficient appliances is explicitly justified using study findings about high-energy-appliance usage and includes brief consideration of emissions impact.  This meets the 3-mark descriptor (well-justified application).

Despite scattered typographical artefacts, arguments progress logically from data to conclusion, but malformed tables disrupt readability and coherence in places.  The work is âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, fitting the 2-mark level.

annotations.comment: Minor wording redundancy in âinfluence differencesâ reduces precision; consider rephrasing the research question to avoid duplication of terms.
The environmental context is thorough but drifts into generic climate facts; tighten focus by incorporating Surabaya-specific emissions or electricity data.
Link between the issue and research question would be stronger with local statistics on household consumption to support claims about Surabaya.
Economic, social, and technological factors are mentioned superficially; deepen the connection with specific policy or demographic evidence for Surabaya.
The title is concise and clearly conveys the study focus on income and energy behaviours in Surabaya, using precise terminology.
The final sentence on survey scope is cut off; complete the thought and clarify how the design addresses the research aims.
Procedure is replicable but omits guidelines for managing missing or partial survey responses; include a data-cleaning protocol.
Sampling via friends and teachers introduces bias; justify this convenience approach or propose stratification to improve representativeness.
The Likert scale is appropriate for frequency measures; consider pilot testing to ensure items function equivalently across income groups.
Justification of one-way ANOVA is sound; add explicit mention of assumption checks (normality, homogeneity of variance) before analysis.
Environmental risks and data security are under-addressed; elaborate on measures for protecting respondentsâ digital data and privacy.
The Tukey HSD figure is useful; ensure axes, group labels, and confidence intervals are legible and annotated for clarity.
Hypotheses are stated clearly, but adding anticipated directionality (e.g., âhigher income â lower consumption scoreâ) would enhance specificity.
Variable classification is comprehensive; strengthen by specifying planned sample sizes per income group to justify the five categories.
Controlled variables ensure consistency, but limiting age to 25â40 may reduce generalizability; discuss this trade-off in the evaluation section.
Referencing processed data is helpful, but summary statistics (means, SDs) should be displayed immediately to guide reader understanding.
ANOVA table for Question 1 is malformed and contains placeholder text; reconstruct output to show actual F- and p-values clearly.
Interpretation of p > 0.05 is correct; consider reporting effect size (e.g., $$\eta^2$$) to convey the magnitude of non-significance.
After rejecting Hâ for high-energy appliances, briefly discuss potential policy actions tailored to Surabaya households for this behaviour.
Avoid duplicate evaluation tables; consolidate strengths and limitations into one section for coherence and to prevent redundancy.
Application proposal is well tied to findings; further evaluate potential rebound effects and outline monitoring strategies for subsidy success.
Good use of pre-paid meter system literature; ensure consistent citation formatting and include more local policy examples for depth.
Bibliography entries are relevant but citation styles vary; apply a consistent format (e.g., APA) throughout to improve professionalism.
Interpretation of group differences is detailed but repetitive; summarize key findings succinctly to improve concision.
ANOVA output for Question 4 contains nonsensical symbols; rerun analysis and ensure software export displays numeric results correctly.
ANOVA table for Question 6 is illegible; replace placeholder symbols with actual F- and p-values to support the conclusion.
Group-level null results are clear; consider summarizing non-significant outcomes in a consolidated table to streamline reading.
Conclusion restates key findings but would benefit from comparing results with literature to provide broader theoretical context.
Limitations table notes focus on income; include discussion of unequal group sizesâ effect on ANOVA validity to fully evaluate method.
Ethical evaluation notes privacy but omit whether IRB or institutional review approval was obtained; clarify research oversight.
Proposed inclusion of actual electricity usage is strong; extend suggestion by outlining mixed-methods integration of meter data with self-reports.
